It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has several distinct objects: - Two "cross" patterns (r dots around B centers) at top-right and bottom-left - A small green container with an r dot inside (top center) - A yellow vertical bar with an r dot inside (left side) - Two large gray containers, each with two compartments containing colored shapes (N and Y) - The only valid action is MOUSE, suggesting I need to click on something
- Goal model: Unclear yet - likely involves moving colored items between containers or activating elements by clicking.
- Plan: Try clicking on the cross pattern center (B pixel at 10, 52) to see if that's interactive.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: This click changed 2 cells. Let me check what changed - was it gameplay or just HUD?

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
before_ascii = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after_ascii = last_transition.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')

changed_cells = []
for r in range(64):
    for c in range(64):
        if r < len(before_ascii) and c < len(before_ascii[r]):
            if r < len(after_ascii) and c < len(after_ascii[r]):
                if before_ascii[r][c] != after_ascii[r][c]:
                    changed_cells.append((r, c, before_ascii[r][c], after_ascii[r][c]))

print("Changed cells:", changed_cells)

# Check the cross pattern area
print("\nCross pattern area (rows 9-12, cols 50-54):")
for r in range(9, 13):
    print(f"  Row {r}: {after_ascii[r][50:55]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:

  Row 10: BrBrB
  Row 11: BBrBB
  Row 12: BBBBB

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Again, only the HUD bar changed (row 63). The cross pattern is still intact. So clicking on the B center of the cross pattern also didn't change gameplay. 
Let me think about what else to try. The result says `gameplay_changed: true` but the only changes are on row 63 (the HUD bar). Wait, that's contradictory - if only the HUD changed, shouldn't `gameplay_changed` be false?
Actually, looking back at the first click result, it also said `gameplay_changed: true` but only changed (63, 63). And the second click changed (63, 61) and (63, 62). So the HUD bar is changing but the system still reports `gameplay_changed: true`. 
Hmm, but the instructions say "the harness learns the game's deterministic HUD/edge band online and tells you whether your action changed real gameplay (gameplay_changed) or only advanced that band (no_impact)". So maybe the HUD model hasn't been fully learned yet?
Let me try clicking on something more substantial - maybe the colored shapes inside the containers, or the container walls themselves. Let me try clicking on the N shape inside the big gray container.
Looking at the ASCII:
- The N shape in the top-left compartment of the big gray container is at roughly rows 20-22, cols 38-40
- The Y shape in the top compartment of the bottom container is at roughly rows 37-39, cols 23-25
Let me try clicking on one of those shapes.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated:
- Both clicks so far only changed the HUD bar at row 63 - no actual gameplay changes
- The system reports `gameplay_changed: true` but only HUD pixels changed, suggesting the HUD model isn't fully learned yet
- Need to find what actually interacts with the puzzle elements
Plan: Try clicking on the colored shapes inside the containers - the N shape at (20, 39) inside the big gray container.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the N shape inside the big gray container (node 9)
# The N shape (node 13) is at boundary [[20, 38], [20, 40], [20, 39], [21, 38], [22, 39], [22, 40], [22, 38]]
# Center is roughly (21, 39)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 21, 'col': 39}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
